column7: message
It is body dysmorphia. It's just that they don't know or don't want to admit they have a mental problem, they describe it as a body problem... so yeah clearly body dysmorphia.
Gender *dysphoria* and body *dysmorphia* are two completely different things.
@intricate-beam, with all due respect, you're oversimplifying and dismissing valid feelings. It's not that simple.
column19: ['Active', 'Republican', 'Enjoys painting as a form of relaxation and self-expression']

column7: "@intricate-beam, let's remember to respect everyone's experiences and avoid making assumptions about others' mental health. @quiet-rail, thank you for clarifying the difference between gender dysphoria and body dysmorphia. @perpendicular-wire, well said. Let's keep the discussion respectful and considerate."

column7:
Iowa and New Hampshire are not very representative of the nation just as Mississippi and Alabama are not very representative of the nation. If Mississippi and Alabama were the first two states to vote, I would expect the Sanders campaign to make a similar statement saying that you have to take the results with a grain of salt.   

As for Hillary winning many southern states, Obama did the same thing in 2008 and it worked out pretty well for him. All votes in all states should count. Do you know what state has the most Republican voters in the country? California. Following your logic, do you think the Republicans should not count the votes of all the California Republicans?
Considering how late California votes in the primary process, they generally do not have an impact.

column7: Your CMV argument itself is flawed. In the title you state that Kpop is morally wrong, whereas in your tldr;, you state that consuming Kpop is morally wrong. You cannot conflate the two, as the former unambiguously implies that the institution of Kpop itself is wrong, whereas the latter specifically embodies a claim that the consumption of Kpop is wrong. The two are very different things.

column7:
> If I saw someone walking down the street with a gun and they shot me, I will probably avoid people carrying guns in the street. It might be unfair to law-abiding gun owners, but the fact that someone has a gun in public is related to my injury. Similarly, the sex of strangers around a woman is related to the type of crime (sexual assault) whereas race is not. 

One major difference between these situations, though, is that you can choose not to wear a gun, whereas you can't choose not to be a man or not to have a certain skin colour...

>you can’t choose not to be a man

I don’t think it matters if you can choose. The fact of the matter is that men are far more likely to assault a woman than another woman, so there is a logical fear. If a woman is afraid of all black men because they remind her of somebody who raped her, that is understandable, but ultimately isn’t grounded in any statistic about sexual assault.

While it is arguably unfair to men, it does make much more sense to be uncomfortable around men in general than it does to be uncomfortable around all black men.

"@frigid-wheel, I understand your point, but I still believe it's important to acknowledge that fear isn't always rational or fair. We can't control our initial reactions, but we should strive to be aware of our biases and not let them dictate our actions. Let's not forget, we're all trying to foster a safer, more inclusive environment."
